does: This paper introduces Safety-Aware Intent Defense (SAID), a training-free defense mechanism against jailbreak attacks on large language models. SAID operates by first distilling potentially obfuscated user inputs into concise core intents using the target model itself, then applying a validated safety prefix to probe whether each distilled intent violates safety guidelines, and finally employing a conservative aggregation rule to reject requests if any intent is identified as unsafe. The method requires no model parameter updates, external filters, or decoding-time modifications, making it compatible with black-box model access while maintaining benign-task utility.
connects: This work builds on a growing body of research addressing adversarial vulnerabilities in aligned LLMs, extending prior defense strategies that rely on external filtering systems, auxiliary guardrails, or decoding-time control mechanisms. It responds to limitations in these existing approaches by proposing an intent-level safety probing strategy that avoids computational overhead and deployment constraints. The research connects to broader work on prompt injection vulnerabilities, safety alignment techniques, and the tension between robustness and utility in LLM defenses. SAID leverages the target model's own capabilities for intent distillation rather than requiring separate safety models or auxiliary systems, drawing on insights about using models for self-directed safety checks.
enables: This approach opens several future research directions for practical safety deployment of LLMs. By demonstrating a training-free, parameter-efficient defense mechanism, SAID enables broader implementation of safety measures across diverse model architectures without retraining costs. The intent distillation framework could inspire further work on understanding and decomposing obfuscated adversarial prompts, potentially leading to better understanding of how models interpret complex user inputs. Future work might extend this to multi-modal systems, investigate adaptive attacks that specifically target intent-level defenses, or develop more sophisticated intent aggregation strategies that balance safety and utility more finely across diverse use cases.
